title: First Encounter
text_warrior:
It happens between one breath and the next.

Saoirse stops mid-sentence — just stops, her mouth still open, her hand on the grip of her sword without the sword moving an inch. Mireille's pen hovers over her notebook and does not descend. Tierlan, who was crouched by the fire coaxing it back to life, stays crouched, frozen in the act of tending something he can no longer tend. Their eyes are open. All three of them. You can see them see you. They cannot move.

She steps out of the dark between the trees as though the dark offered her a door.

Tall. Unhurried. The firelight catches her the way firelight catches something that knows it is being watched and has decided to be worth looking at. Her eyes are not a color that belongs here. Nothing about her is threatening in the way that wolves and cultists are threatening. She is threatening the way a sentence that hasn't finished yet is threatening.

She looks at you with the expression of someone arriving somewhere they have been before.

"You led the Thornfield cadets for two years," she says. Her voice is even, conversational, the tone of a report being read back to you. "Before the posting fell through. Before they stopped sending support. You fought that holding action on the Keld ford alone because there was no one else, and you won, and you never told anyone how close it was. You carry that." A pause. Something in her eyes that is not quite amusement. "I found that very interesting."

She crosses to where you stand and you do not move — not frozen, not compelled, simply not moving, the way you sometimes don't move when something important is about to be decided and you don't want to be the one who decides it wrong.

Her hand settles against your sternum. Palm flat. Deliberate.

"This is mine," she says, and means it the way you would mean a claim on something you had already planned for.

The warmth begins at the point of contact — not heat, not pain, something older than both, pressing through cloth and skin and into the chest cavity like a thumb finding the center of something. You feel the moment it settles. The moment it becomes permanent. There is a sound you do not hear so much as understand.

She withdraws her hand. The warmth does not withdraw. It will not.

"I've been watching this road for a long time," she says, stepping back into the dark with the same unhurried ease she came from it. "You are the most interesting thing to walk it in years." A beat. "I look forward to the rest of it."

And she is gone, and the fire pops once, and Saoirse's hand completes its motion toward the sword that does not need drawing, and Mireille's pen touches the paper and stays there without writing anything, and Tierlan straightens up slowly and looks at the tree line for a very long time.

Nothing about her is threatening in the way that wolves and cultists are threatening. She is threatening the way a sentence that hasn't finished yet is threatening.

She looks at you with the expression of someone arriving somewhere they have been before.

"You read ground the way most people read faces," she says. Her voice is even, as though this is an observation rather than a revelation. "The Dalesmark ambush — you read it three minutes before it happened, didn't you? You just couldn't make them listen in time. You carry that too." A pause. Something in her eyes that is not quite amusement. "You've been reading this forest since you arrived. Tell me: what did you find?"

She doesn't wait for an answer. She crosses to where you stand and you do not move — not frozen, not compelled, simply not moving, the way you sometimes don't move when something important is about to be decided and you don't want to be the one who decides it wrong.

Her hand settles against your sternum. Palm flat. Deliberate.

"This is mine," she says, and means it the way you would mean a claim on something you had already planned for.

The warmth begins at the point of contact — not heat, not pain, something older than both, pressing through cloth and skin and into the chest cavity like a thumb finding the center of something. You feel the moment it settles. The moment it becomes permanent. There is a sound you do not hear so much as understand.

She withdraws her hand. The warmth does not withdraw. It will not.

"I've been watching this road for a long time," she says, stepping back into the dark with the same unhurried ease she came from it. "You are the most interesting thing to walk it in years." A beat. "I look forward to the rest of it."

companion_reactions.saoirse: I've seen paralysis spells. That wasn't a paralysis spell.
companion_reactions.mireille: Mireille writes for a long time without speaking. The entry runs to three pages, then a fourth. At some point she draws a careful outline of the mark's shape from your description, labels the proportions, and underlines the word 'warm' twice.
companion_reactions.tierlan: Tierlan says nothing. He walks the full perimeter of the camp twice, checks the snares he set before dark, and then takes a watch position with his back to the fire so his eyes stay adjusted. He does not look at you directly for the rest of the night.

aftermath: Morning comes gray and cold. Nobody mentions it first. Mireille closes her notebook when you emerge from your bedroll. Saoirse hands you a ration without looking at your chest. The mark is there — you know it is there — warm against the sternum in the early cold like something that has already decided to stay.
